meta.title: Interfacial Electric Fields in Water Nanodroplets are Weakly Dependent on Curvature and pH
meta.note: Computational chemistry / MD paper; main checkable quantities are field magnitudes, simulation parameters, pH/charge values, and fit statistics. Table S* and Fig S* are in unavailable SI.

claims.text: Across planar interfaces and nanodroplets of varying curvature and charge state, an outward-oriented interfacial electric field of ~1.0–1.2 V/Å along the intrinsic surface normal is found.

location.quote: find an outward-oriented field of ∼ 1.0–1.2 V/˚A along the intrinsic surface normal

claims.scope: planar interfaces and nanodroplets, WC surface normal

claims.text: The IEF magnitude scales linearly with the average number of hydrogen bonds per interfacial molecule.

location.quote:
its magnitude scales linearly with the average number of hydrogen bonds per
interfacial molecule

claims.predicate: linear correlation exists between IEF magnitude and average H-bonds per interfacial molecule

claims.text: The IEF changes by a factor of only ~10^-5 between 3 and 40 µm-sized droplets.

location.quote:
which changes by a factor
of only ∼ 10−5 between 3 and 40µm-sized droplets

claims.text: The IEF is localized inside the interfacial region and rapidly vanishes within a few Å.

location.quote:
the IEF is localized
inside the interfacial region and rapidly vanishes within a few ˚A

claims.predicate: IEF decays to ~zero within a few Å from interface

claims.text: The air-water boundary may sustain electric fields on the order of ~0.2 V/Å (prior literature estimate).

location.quote: that the air-water boundary may sustain electric fields on the order of ∼ 0.2 V/˚A

claims.text: The reported IEF magnitude (~1.0–1.2 V/Å) is about 5-6 times larger than previous computational estimates.

location.quote:
i.e., about 5-6 times larger than previous computational
estimates.

claims.predicate: ratio of reported IEF (1.0-1.2 V/Å) to prior estimate (~0.2 V/Å) == ~5-6

claims.text: Each DPMD simulation was carried out in the NVT canonical ensemble for at least 1 ns at 300 K with CSVR thermostat (damping 0.04 ps).

location.quote:
Each DPMD simulation was
carried out in the NVT canonical ensemble for at least 1 ns. The temperature was kept fixed
at 300 K via the canonical-sampling-velocity-rescaling (CSVR) thermostat 55 with a damping
factor of 0.04 ps.

claims.predicate: simulation_time >= 1 ns; T == 300 K; damping == 0.04 ps

claims.text: 500 randomly chosen configurations were extracted from all DPMD trajectories and re-sampled via ab initio PBE+D3 DFT.

location.quote:
500 randomly chosen configurations were extracted from all DPMD trajectories and re-
sampled via ab initio calculations at the PBE+D3

claims.text: Charge states corresponding to -5e, -e, +e, and +5e were simulated, probing 13% and 66% of the Rayleigh limit, corresponding to net pH values of 13.74, 13.0, 0.96, and 0.26.

location.section: pH Effects on the Interfacial Electric Field
location.quote:
charge states corresponding to −5e
−, −e−, +e−, and +5e−, hence probing regimes of 13%
and 66% of the Rayleigh limit for Coulomb fission. These charge states approximately cor-
respond to net pH values of 13.74, 13.0, 0.96, and 0.26, respectively.

claims.text: The IEF linearly increases with the total excess charge.

location.quote: that the IEF linearly increases with the total excess charge

claims.predicate: IEF increases linearly with droplet excess charge

claims.text: In the vapor1 region the typical field values drop to approximately ~0.3 V/Å, while in vapor2 the field vanishes within statistical uncertainty.

location.quote:
In the vapor1 region the typical field values drop to smaller
values of approximately ∼ 0.3 V/˚A, while in the vapor2 region the field vanishes within
the statistical uncertainty.

claims.text: The interfacial-region electric field distributions peak at approximately 1.2–1.4 V/Å.

location.quote:
The corresponding distributions peak at approximately
1.2–1.4 V/˚A

claims.text: Fig 2b shows an anticorrelation between IEF and average H-bond number with R^2=0.994 and Pearson's r=-0.997.
claims.predicate: reported R_squared == 0.994 and Pearson_r == -0.997 are consistent

claims.text: The average dipole angle is α ~ 96° in the droplet and α ~ 94° in the slab, implying a net outward-directed electric field.

location.quote:
leading to an
average α ∼ 96◦in the droplet (α ∼ 94◦in the slab), implies a net electric field directed
outward from the liquid toward the vapor.

claims.text: Extrapolation of the curvature dependence to 3-40 µm droplets reveals variations on the order of 10^-5 V/Å, five orders of magnitude smaller than the field itself.

location.quote:
reveals variations on the order of 10−5 V/˚A, i.e., five orders of magnitude smaller than
the field itself.

claims.text: Benchmark SCAN and r2SCAN calculations yield essentially indistinguishable IEF distributions, demonstrating robustness to the density functional.

location.quote: which yield essentially indistinguishable IEF distributions (see Fig. S8 of the SI)

claims.predicate: IEF distributions at PBE+D3, SCAN, r2SCAN are indistinguishable

claims.text: Flat-interface samples of 256 and 512 H2O were used with box edges a=b=19.641, c=45.000 Å and a=b=24.830, c=60.000 Å respectively.

location.quote:
samples composed of 256 and 512 H2O molecules were considered with box edges of
a = b = 19.641, c = 45.000 ˚A and a = b = 24.830, c = 60.000 ˚A, respectively

claims.predicate: box dimensions as stated for 256 and 512 molecule slabs

claims.text: Seven isolated nanodroplets of 256, 384, 512, 640, 768, 896, 1024 H2O were simulated in cubic boxes with edges 44, 48, 60, 63, 65, 67, 68 Å.

location.quote:
nanodroplets composed of 256, 384, 512, 640, 768, 896, and 1024 H2O molecules were simu-
lated in cubic boxes with edges of 44, 48, 60, 63, 65, 67, and 68 ˚A, respectively

claims.predicate: nanodroplet sizes and box edges as listed
